explanation: The formula specifies that after exactly two time units, prop_0 holds, provided prop_1 holds at the current time. To express 'after 2 time units', we use the LTL 'next' operator twice: X X prop_0. To condition this on prop_1 holding now, use implication: prop_1 -> X X prop_0.

We want to specify that after exactly 2 time units from whenever prop_1 becomes true, prop_0 must hold. The phrase 'after 2 time units prop_1' suggests: whenever prop_1 occurs, two steps later prop_0 must be true. In LTL, this is expressed by: 'whenever prop_1, then in the next of the next state, prop_0'.

The phrase means that once prop_1 becomes true, it is forbidden for prop_0 to occur after exactly 2 time units. In LTL, this means: Whenever prop_1 holds, then in the position that is the second time step afterwards, prop_0 must not occur. This can be encoded as: Globally, if prop_1 holds now, then prop_0 must not occur two steps later.

The sentence says that 'prop_0' must occur at some point exactly two time units after 'prop_1'. In LTL, since we cannot use numerical indices for time, we check that if 'prop_1' holds now, then 'prop_0' must hold two steps later (i.e., after two X operators). Since we are not allowed to use numerical comparison, we must directly express the distance as chaining X operators: whenever 'prop_1' occurs, in two steps 'prop_0' must be true.

The statement says that once prop_1 becomes true, then for the following 2 time units, prop_0 cannot happen. Only after these 2 steps can prop_0 occur. In LTL, this can be modeled by saying: whenever prop_1 is true at time t, prop_0 must be false at time t+1 and t+2. Using the X (next) operator: G(prop_1 -> (!X prop_0 & !XX prop_0)).

The statement requires that after prop_1 becomes true, prop_0 must not occur for at least the next 2 time units (that is, for the immediate next and the following time instant). In LTL, we express this by saying: 'whenever prop_1 occurs, then for the next 2 time units, prop_0 is false.' We can use the G (globally) operator to cover every occurrence. For the 'next 2 time units', we encode 'not prop_0' now and also at the next time instant: !(prop_0) & X!(prop_0).

The sentence means that once prop_1 holds, prop_0 cannot hold after two time units. In LTL, we enforce that whenever prop_1 is true at a time point, it is not possible for prop_0 to hold at that point, or at the next time point, or at the next-next (second) time point. That is: for all time steps, if prop_1 holds then prop_0 is not true at the 2nd next step.

The statement says prop_0 cannot occur after 2 time units once prop_1 has occurred. This can be expressed by saying: Once prop_1 becomes true, in all paths, prop_0 is not allowed from the second moment after prop_1 onward. In LTL, without numerical comparisons, this means: whenever prop_1 holds, in the next state it is not prop_0, and in the next of the next state, globally, prop_0 never holds. Thus: G(prop_1 -> (X (!prop_0) & XX (G !prop_0))).

The constraint 'A DM cannot prop_0 after 2 time units prop_1' means: after prop_1 becomes true, for the next 2 time units, prop_0 is allowed, but from the third time unit onward, prop_0 must not occur. In LTL, 'from the third step after prop_1, globally no prop_0' is captured as: globally, if prop_1 holds, then X(X(G(!prop_0))) (i.e., from two steps after prop_1, always not prop_0).
After exactly two steps in the future, if prop_1 holds at that time, then prop_0 must also hold.
